response:
## Decision: O4 — Pre-registered sequential escalation

**Adopt O4.** Run the full pre-registered base grid (4 arms × 8 tasks × 3 reps, per Comment 1's "Method (lean)" section), analyze against the pre-registered decision rule, and invoke the escalation clause — "add reps/tasks only where a CI straddles the decision boundary; stop when unambiguous" — exactly as Comment 1's Analysis section specifies, if the base pass leaves the Tiered-vs-Sonnet-solo boundary ambiguous.

## Rationale

**Unanimous, high-confidence advisor signal.** All three advisors scored O4 at 5/5 with no dissent, versus O1 at 2-3, and O2/O3 at 1-2. No advisor identified a premise that currently holds and would reverse O4 downward.

**O4 is literal execution of Comment 1's pre-registration, not a new design.** The Analysis section pre-registers escalation as part of the protocol; Method fixes 3 reps as the base (not an invented "below-3 minimal" start, per Advisor 3's reversal check — no such threshold needs inventing). O4 = run the pre-registered base grid, then apply the pre-registered stopping rule. This is the one option that implements *both* the Method and Analysis sections of Comment 1 in full.

**O1 silently drops half the pre-registration.** Running the full grid "before any analysis" and stopping there (O1) ignores the escalation clause Comment 1 already committed to. If the base-grid CI straddles the boundary, O1 ships an inconclusive result with a pre-registered resolution path available and unused — that's exactly the kind of process-gate omission this repo's muda discipline flags. Advisor 2's crux — that Comment 3's later 250-300-invocation estimate might supersede the escalation clause — doesn't hold up against Comment 3's own framing: it's headed "the issue's pre-registration validated against actual code," i.e. a feasibility/execution check (grader independence, hermetic controls, retry format), not a redesign of the Analysis section. Silence on escalation in an execution-mechanics comment isn't a repeal of a stats method a different comment already pre-registered; ~250-300 is a sane upper-bound budget estimate, not a mandate to spend it all unconditionally.

**O2/O3 fail on their own terms.** Both pilot a subset of arms and both were scored 1-2 by every advisor because they sacrifice pre-registered required deliverables: the Haiku-solo/Opus-solo task-gradient pre-screen and the "Opus-quality at sub-Opus-cost" prize report (explicitly flagged as an owner-mandated output in Comment 1's Decision rule paragraph). O3 additionally can't even evaluate its own target — Opus-solo isn't a term in the Tiered-vs-Sonnet-solo decision rule, so an Opus+Sonnet-solo pilot can't make that gate decidable regardless of outcome. Stopping after the two arms most favorable to an early "yes" (O2) is also close to the optional-stopping/fishing bias Comment 1 opens by saying pre-registration exists "to avoid fishing for a favorable cut" — cherry-picking which arms to run is a subtler version of the same hazard the pre-registration was built to prevent.

**This choice is muda-consistent.** Deleting the escalation step (O1) forgoes cost savings the "Method (lean)" framing was explicitly built for. Skipping arms (O2/O3) doesn't save process — it produces a report with holes that then need a second round to fill, which is worse than running the grid once with a built-in early-stop.

## Consequences
- Execution: run all 4 arms × 8 pre-screened tasks × 3 reps first (per Comment 1's Method). Compute the eval-clustered 95% CI on Tiered − Sonnet-solo. If the CI does not straddle the -0.05 boundary, stop and apply the decision rule as-is. If it straddles, add reps/tasks per the escalation clause until unambiguous, then re-apply the decision rule.
- The tiered-orchestration script (Comment 3, new code) still needs `model:` set per role per the #53 lesson before any of this runs.
- Comment 3's three owner-calls (grader independence residual, which hermetic controls carry over, retry-cap/feedback format) are prerequisites to writing that script and are unaffected by this execution-design decision — they still need resolving before spend, independent of O1-O4.

options.label: Full pre-registered grid
options.summary: Run all 4 arms (Haiku-solo, Sonnet-solo, Opus-solo, Tiered) across all 8 pre-screened tasks at 3 reps each before any analysis.

options.label: Pilot Tiered arm first
options.summary: Run Tiered vs Sonnet-solo baseline first at pre-registered reps; check the delta/token decision rule; stop before scaling other arms if already decidable.

options.label: Pilot Opus-solo first
options.summary: Run the costliest-per-invocation arm, Opus-solo, plus Sonnet-solo baseline first to test reachability of the 'Opus-quality at sub-Opus-cost' prize before full-grid spend.

options.label: Pre-registered sequential escalation
options.summary: Run all 4 arms at minimal reps first, adding reps/tasks only where a CI straddles the decision boundary, per Comment 1's stated escalation method.

O1.crux: Matches Comment 1's base 3-rep x 4-arm grid but drops the pre-registered escalation clause that resolves ambiguous CIs.
O1.dependency: If the base 3-rep grid already yields a decisive (non-straddling) CI for the Tiered-vs-Sonnet-solo decision rule, O1 is equivalent to O4 and should score higher; if the CI straddles the boundary at 3 reps, O1 ships an inconclusive result with no pre-registered path to resolve it, and the score should fall.

O2.crux: Cheaply targets the primary decision rule (Tiered delta/tokens vs Sonnet-solo) but abandons the pre-registered 4-arm design and the 'avoid fishing' rationale by stopping after the two arms most favorable to an early yes.
O2.dependency: If the Opus-ceiling and Haiku-floor comparisons are truly optional/secondary deliverables that don't affect the go/no-go validity (i.e., the decision rule alone is a complete, self-sufficient adoption test per Comment 1's 'Decision rule' paragraph), then O2's shortcut is legitimate and the score should reverse upward; if the honest-cost frontier and task-gradient claims require all 4 arms to be credible, O2 stays wrong.

O3.crux: Gates spend on the 'real prize' (Opus-quality at sub-Opus-cost), a secondary reported outcome, rather than the primary decision rule, and delays building the actual tiered-orchestration script where the real engineering risk and the question's real point sit.
O3.dependency: If reaching Opus-solo quality is actually a necessary precondition for the Tiered arm to be worth building at all (i.e., without demonstrated Sonnet/Opus divergence on these tasks, Tiered has no ceiling to chase), piloting Opus-solo first is a legitimate cheap gate and the score should reverse upward; if the pre-registered decision rule (Tiered vs Sonnet-solo) is independently sufficient regardless of the Opus ceiling, O3 misprioritizes and stays wrong.

O4.crux: This is verbatim the escalation method Comment 1 pre-registers under Analysis ('add reps/tasks only where a CI straddles the decision boundary; stop when unambiguous'), and Comment 3 explicitly reaffirms running 'per Comment 1's stated escalation method' — no deviation from pre-registration.
O4.dependency: If Comment 1's 'sequential escalation' line is actually a note about how to treat already-collected data during analysis (not an instruction to run arms at literally minimal reps before the pre-registered 3-rep base), then O4's framing of 'minimal reps first' mischaracterizes the method — since the Method section already fixes 3 reps as the base, not a minimal starting point — and the score should fall toward O1's.

O1.crux: Comment 1's Analysis section pre-registers sequential escalation ('add reps/tasks only where a CI straddles the boundary; stop when unambiguous'), so running the full 3-rep/4-arm/8-task grid unconditionally contradicts the registered protocol and forfeits its built-in cost savings.
O1.dependency: If Comment 3's later draft (3 reps x 8 tasks x 4 arms, ~250-300 invocations, no mention of escalation) is treated as superseding Comment 1's escalation clause as the operative pre-registration, full-grid becomes the correct reading and the score rises.

O2.crux: Piloting only Tiered vs Sonnet-solo skips Haiku-solo and Opus-solo, which the pre-registration needs for the task-gradient pre-screen and for reporting reachability of the 'Opus-quality at sub-Opus cost' prize (owner call flags this explicitly), so an early Tiered-vs-Sonnet decision would ship without data the pre-reg promised.
O2.dependency: If the Haiku/Opus pre-screen was already completed as a separate prior 1-rep pass and the only remaining open question is the core Tiered-vs-Sonnet hypothesis, this pilot loses nothing required and the score rises.

O3.crux: Neither the pre-registered decision rule nor 'the real prize' (Opus-quality at sub-Opus cost) can be evaluated from Opus-solo + Sonnet-solo alone, since both hinge on the Tiered arm's performance, which this pilot never runs.
O3.dependency: If the actual near-term goal is a go/no-go gate on whether to even build the new tiered-orchestration script (i.e., first confirm an Opus-quality ceiling exists worth chasing) rather than deciding the tiering hypothesis itself, an Opus-solo-first pilot is a legitimate cheap gate and the score rises.

O4.crux: This is a near-verbatim restatement of Comment 1's pre-registered 'Sequential escalation' clause, and uniquely keeps all 4 arms in play so the task-gradient pre-screen and prize-comparison data are never sacrificed.
O4.dependency: If minimal-rep runs across 4 arms x 8 tasks are too noisy to ever produce a decidable CI before hitting the full 3 reps anyway, escalation buys no savings over O1 and just adds process overhead, reversing the score downward.

O1.crux: Matches the literal 3-rep×4-arm×8-task base design and avoids any stopping-bias, but ignoring Comment 1's own pre-registered sequential-escalation clause forgoes the cost savings the 'Method (lean)' framing was built for.
O1.dependency: If 'before any analysis' means only the first pass — compatible with, not a substitute for, later escalation — this rises to 4-5; it only stays a 3 if it forecloses ever invoking the escalation clause.

O2.crux: Directly pilots the exact pair (Tiered vs Sonnet-solo) the pre-registered decision rule is written against, but stopping there abandons two required deliverables — Haiku-solo's role in the gradient and Opus-solo's 'real prize'/frontier reporting — that Comment 1 mandates regardless of the primary verdict.
O2.dependency: Reverses to 4-5 if the owner's only goal is the binary adopt/reject call and the frontier + Opus-prize report are dispensable rather than pre-registered required outputs.

O3.crux: Opus-solo isn't a term in the pre-registered decision rule (Tiered vs Sonnet-solo delta/tokens), so piloting it first can't make that gate decidable — it answers the secondary 'real prize' question while leaving the primary hypothesis untested.
O3.dependency: Reverses to 4-5 if 'Opus-quality at sub-Opus cost' is actually the primary goal driving the issue (not a secondary report), making Opus reachability the one fact that could kill the project outright before other spend.

O4.crux: Not a new design choice but literal execution of Comment 1's own pre-registered Analysis clause ('add reps/tasks only where a CI straddles the decision boundary; stop when unambiguous'), capturing early-stop cost savings without the optional-stopping bias that skipping whole arms (O2/O3) would introduce.
O4.dependency: Reverses to 3 or lower if 'minimal reps first' can't be cleanly operationalized — Comment 1 never specifies a below-3 starting rep count, so implementing this requires inventing a threshold that isn't actually pre-registered.

O1.crux: Matches Comment 1's base 3-rep x 4-arm grid but drops the pre-registered escalation clause that resolves ambiguous CIs.
O1.dependency: If the base 3-rep grid already yields a decisive (non-straddling) CI for the Tiered-vs-Sonnet-solo decision rule, O1 is equivalent to O4 and should score higher; if the CI straddles the boundary at 3 reps, O1 ships an inconclusive result with no pre-registered path to resolve it, and the score should fall.
